content: Hey Saturday, I’m finally building a little home server and want a sanity check. I’ve got an Intel N100 mini PC with 32GB RAM and a 2TB NVMe, and I’m planning to run Proxmox VE with VMs for Home Assistant, Plex, and an Nginx reverse proxy. Does that sound reasonable?

Yep, that’s a solid setup for those workloads. The N100 is efficient and 32GB gives you enough headroom for all three VMs plus Proxmox overhead. I’d just allocate resources conservatively at first and scale up after monitoring usage for a week or two.

Nice. For networking, I was thinking Nginx + Cloudflare Tunnel so I don’t have to open ports on my router. I mostly need remote access for Home Assistant dashboards and maybe a couple internal web tools.

That’s a great approach and much safer than exposing ports directly. Run cloudflared in its own small VM or container and lock tunnel routes to only the hostnames you need (like ha.yourdomain.com). Also enable Cloudflare Access or at least MFA for anything sensitive.
Good call. I also want backups automated to Backblaze B2—ideally nightly with some retention policy. Should I back up at the VM level from Proxmox, or inside each VM too?

Do both: Proxmox VM backups for fast full restores, plus app-level backups for critical data like Home Assistant configs and Plex metadata. A common setup is nightly snapshots to local storage, then sync to B2 with pruning (for example: 7 daily, 4 weekly, 6 monthly). If you use Proxmox Backup Server, it makes dedupe and retention much easier.
Perfect. Last thing—rough starting resource split? I was thinking Home Assistant 2 vCPU/4GB, Plex 4 vCPU/8GB, Nginx+cloudflared 1 vCPU/1GB, and leave the rest to Proxmox/ZFS cache.

That’s a very sensible starting point on an N100 box. If Plex does transcoding, watch CPU closely and prefer direct play where possible; otherwise 4 vCPU is usually fine for light use. Leave some RAM unassigned (like 8–10GB) for host overhead and filesystem cache, then tune after real-world usage.
